questions.title: Select All Columns
questions.description: Write a SQL query to select all columns from the 'employees' table.
questions.inputFormat: No input required. Assume table 'employees' exists with columns: id, name, department, salary.
questions.outputFormat: Return all rows and columns from the employees table.
questions.explanation: Use SELECT * to select all columns from a table. The asterisk (*) is a wildcard that represents all columns.
hints: Use SELECT * to get all columns
Specify the table name after FROM

questions.title: Select Specific Columns
questions.description: Write a SQL query to select only the 'name' and 'salary' columns from the 'employees' table.
questions.inputFormat: No input required. Assume table 'employees' exists with columns: id, name, department, salary.
questions.outputFormat: Return name and salary for all employees.
questions.explanation: Specify column names separated by commas in the SELECT statement to retrieve only specific columns.
hints: List column names separated by commas
Order of columns in output matches SELECT order

questions.title: Filter with WHERE Clause
questions.description: Write a SQL query to select all employees from the 'employees' table who work in the 'IT' department.
questions.inputFormat: No input required. Assume table 'employees' exists with columns: id, name, department, salary.
questions.outputFormat: Return all columns for employees in IT department.
questions.constraints: Table contains 1 to 1000 rows. Department values are strings.
questions.explanation: Use the WHERE clause to filter rows based on a condition. String literals must be enclosed in single quotes.

questions.title: Order By Clause
questions.description: Write a SQL query to select all employees from the 'employees' table ordered by salary in descending order (highest salary first).
questions.inputFormat: No input required. Assume table 'employees' exists with columns: id, name, department, salary.
questions.outputFormat: Return all columns sorted by salary descending.
questions.constraints: Table contains 1 to 1000 rows. Salary is a numeric column.

examples.output: SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;

questions.explanation: Use ORDER BY clause to sort results. DESC keyword sorts in descending order. ASC is default for ascending.

testCases.output: SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;

questions.solution: SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;
hints: DESC for descending, ASC for ascending
ORDER BY is usually the last clause

questions.title: Aggregate Function with Group By
questions.description: Write a SQL query to find the average salary for each department from the 'employees' table. Display department and average salary columns.
questions.inputFormat: No input required. Assume table 'employees' exists with columns: id, name, department, salary.
questions.outputFormat: Return department and average salary grouped by department.
questions.constraints: Table contains 1 to 1000 rows. Salary is numeric.

examples.output: SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department;

questions.explanation: Use AVG() aggregate function to calculate average. GROUP BY groups rows with same department values together.

testCases.output: SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department;
SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department;
